parts.content: Outline four considerations a designer should account for when using the $5^{\text{th}}$--$95^{\text{th}}$ percentile range.
parts.markscheme:
- The range intentionally excludes 10% of the population (the smallest 5% and largest 5%), requiring the designer to consider the impact on these excluded users; <marks>1</marks>
- To accommodate the significant size variation within this 90% of the population, the product often requires adjustable features (e.g., adjustable car seat height); <marks>1</marks>
- The $5^{\text{th}}$--$95^{\text{th}}$ percentile range is used as a compromise between the high cost of universal design and the limited utility of designing for the "average" user; <marks>1</marks>
- The nature of the dimension being measured (e.g., functional fit versus clearance), as this range is most appropriate for dimensions involving adjustability; <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

questions.specification: A designer developing safety equipment for workers in extreme environments needs to consider physiological ergonomics primarily to:

options.content: Minimize fatigue and physical strain during prolonged use
Ensure products are aesthetically pleasing to users
Understand how form affects emotional response
Reduce cognitive workload under time pressure

questions.specification: Bicycle design and anthropometric data

parts.content: Describe how both static and dynamic anthropometric data are used in the design of a bicycle.
parts.markscheme:
- **Describe** how static data (e.g. leg length, arm reach) is used to determine the fixed dimensions of the bicycle frame and handlebar position <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** how seat height is designed based on static leg extension requirements to ensure an ergonomic fit and efficient power transfer <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** how dynamic anthropometric data (e.g. user motion ranges during pedaling or steering) is used to ensure mechanical clearance and functionality <marks>A1</marks>
- **Describe** how the application of dynamic data ensures user comfort through the full range of movement and prevents joint strain <marks>A1</marks>
- **Explain** how performance is enhanced by aligning the user's ergonomic posture with the bicycle's motion paths <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** how the integrated use of both data types minimizes the risk of fatigue and repetitive stress injury (RSI) <marks>A1</marks>

Award [1] per relevant application or implication of static/dynamic data, up to [6 max].

6 marks total

questions.specification: A product design team has developed a premium home office setup aimed at hybrid workers. It includes an ergonomic office chair, a height-adjustable sit-stand desk, and a keyboard and mouse system designed to reduce strain and improve posture. The designers aim to meet ergonomic standards across a wide user range. See **Figures 1, 2 and 3**.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A1.1 Ergonomics > A1.1.1 Ergonomics
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.1 Ergonomics
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The entire question revolves around the application of ergonomic principles to a specific set of home office products, evaluating comfort, posture, and efficiency.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A1.1 Ergonomics > A1.1.4 Adjustability and range of sizes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.4 Adjustability and range of sizes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) specifically address how the adjustability of the chair and desk accommodates a wide range of users, which is a core concept of this topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A1.1 Ergonomics > A1.1.6 Physiology
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.6 Physiology
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c) and (e) explicitly ask for physiological benefits and considerations, focusing on how the design reduces physical strain and supports the body.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A1.1 Ergonomics > A1.1.2 Anthropometrics
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.2 Anthropometrics
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) explicitly requires the application of anthropometric data to the design of the keyboard and mouse system.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A1.1 Ergonomics > A1.1.7 Psychology
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.7 Psychology
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) specifically asks for the psychological benefits of using a height-adjustable desk, such as improved focus or well-being.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A. Design in theory > A1.1 Ergonomics > A1.1.5 Workspace envelopes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A1.1.5 Workspace envelopes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) discusses visual ergonomics and the positioning of the monitor arm, which relates to the user's workspace envelope and field of vision.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**

![Ergonomic office chair for hybrid workers](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b176a7e7-0b2d-4d07-918c-ba896b4e2e0b)

Identify two ergonomic features of the chair that enhance comfort.
parts.markscheme:
- Adjustable lumbar support <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Armrest height adjustment <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Any other valid ergonomic feature, e.g. seat pan tilt <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Any other valid ergonomic feature, e.g. mesh backrest <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how adjustability in the chair design supports a wider range of users.
parts.markscheme:
- Adjustability allows for different heights and leg lengths <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps users maintain correct posture <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports 5th–95th percentile range <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2**

![Height-adjustable desk with monitor arm](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9c5190f3-dbb4-43e0-aaf8-35ad4e188817)

Outline two physiological and two psychological benefits of a height-adjustable desk for users.
parts.markscheme:
- **Physiological:** Reduces muscle fatigue and improves circulation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Physiological:** Encourages varied postures (sitting and standing) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Psychological:** Increases sense of autonomy or energy at work <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Psychological:** May improve mental alertness and motivation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how the monitor arm in Figure 2 can be adjusted for optimal visual ergonomics.
parts.markscheme:
- Monitor arm allows screen to be positioned at eye level <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces neck strain from poor angles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Distance can be adjusted to reduce eye fatigue <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows screen tilt or rotation for optimal user preference <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how anthropometric and physiological considerations are applied in the design of the keyboard and mouse.
parts.markscheme:
- **Anthropometric:** Key spacing fits average finger width <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Wrist rest matches hand size and placement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Physiological:** Reduces carpal tunnel strain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Curved mouse shape fits hand grip <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Textured grips improve control and reduce fatigue <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Keyboard angle reduces strain on wrist tendons <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award at least 2 marks from each category <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Referencing **Figures 1**, **2**, and **3**, evaluate how the combination of the chair, desk, and keyboard/mouse system demonstrates the application of ergonomic principles in a modern home office setup.
parts.markscheme:
- Chair and desk support correct posture in sitting and standing positions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- All components are adjustable, catering to percentile ranges <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Wrist support and screen placement reduce physiological strain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Design encourages prolonged, comfortable use <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aesthetics and user-friendly design may boost psychological comfort <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overall, the system shows integrated ergonomic thinking <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award up to 6 marks for a well-balanced evaluation referencing all components <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
What aspect of ergonomics does this highlight?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcq1IIRCCKQfAr11Hv331ZGv4snQPRmOMcNVGrfTzt63MN6g_wRIYkcwktKpE7xfa_cM4rz2i3xT_ztGEH7XKV1rEV_7_l4yfteOtWcQoKdQI3WlT4qMvmd9WA6ZLwMiPxnCYSmJA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Aesthetic mismatch
Overhead lighting design
Poor reach envelope consideration
Screen placement

questions.specification: What is psychology most concerned with in user-centred design?

options.content: Signal processing in smart systems
Study of how users think and sense products
Data encryption for user safety
Muscle activation thresholds

parts.content: Explain how static anthropometric data can be used to design a classroom chair.
parts.markscheme:
- State that static anthropometric data includes measurements such as seated height, thigh length, and hip width <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explain how these measurements help determine the height of the chair, depth of the seat, and width between armrests <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Identify that this ensures comfort and posture support for the majority of students <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Conclude that this minimizes fatigue and supports correct ergonomic positioning <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each valid point related to anthropometric application in chair design, up to [4 max].</note>

questions.specification: What distinguishes adjustability from adaptability in product design?

options.content: Adjustability allows users to tailor product dimensions; adaptability refers to the product’s ability to respond to different user needs or contexts.
options.markscheme: Adjustability refers to the ability of a product to be changed in size or position to accommodate the user (often based on anthropometric percentiles). Adaptability is the extent to which a product can be used in different contexts or for different purposes.

options.content: Adjustability is based on anthropometric data; adaptability is purely aesthetic.
Adjustability accommodates different environments; adaptability accommodates different tasks.
Adjustability refers to fixed settings; adaptability requires manual user customization.

questions.specification:
What is the most likely ergonomic issue in this image?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXf5YjkePOFtoQuYFwbLH--hpHS8e3MBDJEE_kd_BJwCUL0h8yh8M4jeyzR0bRjG_oQVIY9H9svQBUGfHI5hh7hTNaeVB4pSJgZu6jv0v4pt7hSYLEKz9KV_K6gSGlZQrsUEL1sJcw?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Excessive eye strain
options.markscheme: While possible during laptop use, eye strain is a physiological symptom and cannot be directly observed from the physical posture shown in the image.

options.content: Incorrect arm height causing discomfort
options.markscheme: The image shows the user's forearms angled upwards to reach the laptop keyboard. Ergonomic standards suggest that the keyboard should be at a height where the forearms are parallel to the floor to prevent strain in the wrists and shoulders.

options.content: No backrest
options.markscheme: The chair in the image clearly includes a backrest, although the user may not be utilizing it correctly.

options.content: Limited screen brightness
options.markscheme: Screen brightness is an environmental or software setting that cannot be determined from a static image of a person's posture.

questions.specification: A designer is developing a tool for repetitive assembly-line use. Which of the following considerations most directly relates to the biomechanical factor of duration?

options.content: Ensuring the tool remains aesthetically appealing over time
Reducing the time taken to learn how to use the tool
Minimizing sustained muscle exertion over long periods
Designing the tool to be stored efficiently when not in use

questions.specification: Which of the following best represents a user-centred research method?

options.content: Focus groups of target users discussing product interaction
Simulation of supply chain logistics
Analysis of sales data only
Stress testing of material resilience

questions.specification: A design team is developing a universal-access kitchen set that prioritises comfort, safety, and efficiency. The products include a knife with an ergonomic handle, an adjustable-height countertop for seated or standing users, and an overhead cabinet with a pull-down mechanism. The aim is to reduce strain, accommodate various user needs, and promote inclusive kitchen environments.

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7d7b75b1-fc14-4261-8d40-109a2f1072e3)

Identify two ergonomic features of the kitchen knife that reduce user strain.
parts.markscheme:
- Curved handle to reduce wrist strain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Textured or rubberised grip for better control <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how anthropometric data informs the design of the knife handle.
parts.markscheme:
- Handle diameter based on palm width (5th–95th percentile) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Length accommodates both small and large hand sizes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Placement of finger guards or ridges supports secure grip <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/430a14a2-736b-462b-b946-ec760c77d07e)

Explain two benefits of the adjustable countertop for users with differing physical abilities.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows both wheelchair users and standing users to work comfortably <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces back strain for taller/shorter users <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances kitchen safety through posture support <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages independence for people with limited mobility <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the physiological and psychological benefits of enabling users to switch between seated and standing food preparation.
parts.markscheme:
- Physiological: Reduces static load on muscles and joints <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows blood circulation to improve by alternating postures <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Psychological: Increases user autonomy and dignity <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces fatigue and frustration during prolonged tasks <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Discuss how physiological and anthropometric considerations influence the design of the pull-down mechanism.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f87baec5-fcb3-4fc7-a70f-55e9d2b42788)
parts.markscheme:
- Pull-down mechanism reduces need for overextension of arms/shoulders <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smooth resistance avoids sudden movement or strain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Handles shaped for easy grip and operation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Positioning reachable by 5th percentile seated users <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pull cord or bar sized for different hand spans <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Vertical and horizontal reach limits accounted for <marks>1 mark</marks>
- *Award up to [6 max] for a well-balanced evaluation referencing both physical and anthropometric considerations*

parts.content: Referencing **Figures 1**, **2**, and **3**, evaluate how the combination of the knife, countertop, and cabinet promotes an inclusive and ergonomic kitchen environment.
parts.markscheme:
- Knife reduces wrist fatigue and risk of injury during cutting <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Countertop adjustability supports inclusive usability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pull-down cabinet improves access for elderly and disabled users <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Integrating all three elements promotes an inclusive workspace <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Addresses anthropometric and physiological needs holistically <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aesthetics and ease of use may improve user confidence <marks>1 mark</marks>
- *Award up to [6 max] for a well-balanced evaluation referencing all components.*

parts.content: Discuss the limitations of using only anthropometric data in ergonomic design.
parts.markscheme:
- Anthropometric data represents averages and may not suit every user, potentially excluding those at the extremes of the population <marks>1</marks>
- Data does not consider user capabilities such as strength or dexterity, which are often required for effective interaction <marks>1</marks>
- Psychological and physiological factors are excluded from purely physical dimensions, ignoring aspects like user motivation or stress <marks>1</marks>
- Cultural and gender differences may affect the relevance of data if the sampled population does not match the target audience <marks>1</marks>
- Anthropometric data is often static and does not account for dynamic tasks or how user behavior changes during product interaction <marks>1</marks>
- Designing only for physical dimensions may result in products that are technically "fitting" but fail to provide user comfort or satisfaction <marks>1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each valid limitation clearly explained, up to [6 max].</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following design decisions most clearly demonstrates the application of inclusive design principles using anthropometric data?

options.content: Creating product packaging based on Western market dimensions
Offering a single unisex size for speed and simplicity
Designing kitchen counters adjustable for both wheelchair and standing users
Using average male arm length to determine handle positions

parts.content: Discuss how physiological and psychological ergonomic considerations influence the design of a control room for air traffic controllers.
parts.markscheme:
**Physiological:**
- Seating must support long-duration sitting with lumbar support and adjustability <marks>1</marks>
- Displays positioned to reduce eye strain and neck movement <marks>1</marks>
- Lighting designed to reduce glare and fatigue <marks>1</marks>

**Psychological:**
- Interface must reduce cognitive overload with intuitive layout <marks>1</marks>
- Color coding and icons aid fast recognition <marks>1</marks>
- Auditory cues support real-time alerts without distraction <marks>1</marks>

**Combined/General factors:**
- Must support high concentration and mental resilience during long shifts <marks>1</marks>
- Noise reduction in environment to avoid stress <marks>1</marks>
- Ergonomic design contributes to user wellbeing and operational efficiency <marks>1</marks>

*Award [1] for each distinct physiological or psychological ergonomic factor clearly linked to control room design, up to [9 max].*

questions.specification:
What type of anthropometric data is shown here?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfa_P1A2pRNjCe5bwq9ZJpWSPIcpuZHdTyHJFj4-7QnntbwrUSpWFiVXE0NvMoaCp9_Jp9rBLRCVC8a1rvlGKI4DVfa1V7_kzFK7MMNQLSoC4GiRjVT7jqmyc_0ZikKTgN0QJR1QA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Biometric calibration
Psychological profiling
Hand length percentiles
Cognitive load mapping

parts.content: Explain how workspace envelopes are applied in kitchen design.
parts.markscheme:
- Define the 3D space within a user’s reach from a fixed position, both standing and seated, to determine the layout of kitchen components <marks>1</marks>
- Ensure essential items like drawers, cabinets, and appliances are positioned within safe and comfortable reach zones <marks>1</marks>
- Reduce the risk of physical strain or injury by minimizing overextension and unsafe lifting during tasks <marks>1</marks>
- Enhance workflow efficiency by organizing tools and storage within accessible zones relative to the user <marks>1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each relevant ergonomic application of workspace envelopes, up to [4 max].</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following factors is **least** influenced by psychological ergonomics?

options.content: The placement of emergency stop buttons
The tensile strength of a structural component
The perceived safety of using a complex product
The intuitiveness of user interface icons

questions.specification: How should anthropometric data be selected for a general user population?

options.content: By using only the 99th percentile
By choosing values that suit the middle majority
By using average (50th percentile) dimensions only
By selecting only elite athlete data

questions.specification: A product design company is launching a new range of ergonomic home office tools for users working remotely. The range includes an adjustable laptop stand, a contoured vertical mouse, and a footrest with textured massage zones. These products aim to enhance comfort and reduce strain during extended desk work.

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c1bab705-f3a6-4bf8-adfc-2941e7d08a61)

Explain how anthropometric data would influence the height and tilt design of the laptop stand.
parts.markscheme:
- Anthropometric elbow height and eye level guide the tilt and elevation range. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The design ensures neutral neck and wrist posture. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify two potential physiological issues the laptop stand aims to reduce and explain how.
parts.markscheme:
- Neck strain: Raised screen avoids downward tilt of head. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Wrist fatigue: External keyboard placement improves wrist angle. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Back pain: Better posture from upright viewing angle. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Eye strain: Adjustable distance improves viewing ergonomics. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/35b2b35e-24da-411a-9250-934c5c3508c9)

Outline two ergonomic benefits of designing a mouse in a vertical orientation.
parts.markscheme:
- Promotes neutral wrist posture to prevent carpal tunnel. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces forearm pronation and muscular strain. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the mouse’s button placement demonstrates understanding of static anthropometric data.
parts.markscheme:
- Button spacing and angle match hand dimensions from static anthropometric datasets. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Minimizes overextension of fingers. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/decf136d-a8af-4f63-8825-62cd940de91f)

Explain how the inclusion of massage zones on the footrest supports psychological comfort.
parts.markscheme:
- Textured zones stimulate circulation, providing sensory feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The massage zones promote relaxation and reduce mental fatigue. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how this footrest meets both physiological and psychological ergonomic goals for remote workers.
parts.markscheme:
- Physiological: Elevates feet to improve posture and blood flow. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces lower back tension and swelling. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Textured surface reduces static positioning, encouraging micro-movements. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Psychological: Users feel cared for and supported. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces stress with calming tactile experience. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enhances satisfaction and long-term productivity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What does the term “work envelope” refer to in human factors design?

options.content: The 3D space within which a person can reach and manipulate objects
options.markscheme: A work envelope is defined as the 3D space within which a person can reach and manipulate objects to perform a task while in a fixed position. <marks>1</marks>

options.content: The air space needed for ventilation
The psychological boundary of attention
The outer casing of an electronic component

questions.specification: Which of the following scenarios best exemplifies a mismatch in anthropometric design?

options.content: A doorway that accommodates both wheelchairs and strollers
A touch screen that is unresponsive to gloved hands
A shelf that requires most users to stretch above shoulder height to reach
A user interface with excessive screen brightness

questions.specification: Why might a designer deliberately use the 5th percentile when designing a seat height for a public bus?

options.content: To reduce material costs
To allow shorter individuals to sit comfortably and safely
To meet the requirements of average global standards
To ensure taller users can see clearly over other passengers

questions.specification:
What human factor is being monitored in the image?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/1eb17639-0d5a-4663-8034-3520528be1ad.jpeg)

options.content: Psychological cues only
Voice biometrics
Physiological responses
options.markscheme: Physiological responses refer to the monitoring of bodily functions such as heart rate, brain activity (EEG), and skin conductivity. This allows designers to understand how a user's body reacts to a product or environment. The image shows a person with sensors attached for such monitoring.

options.content: Colour theory preferences

questions.specification:
FlexiSpot is designing a sit–stand workstation system. Research shows prolonged sitting causes health issues, while pilot testing shows users experience leg fatigue and back pain when standing. 

Most users alternate between sitting and standing during the day. The system must support both modes effectively.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0ba1f12d-17b8-4d38-830f-acfd2279a5d4)

parts.content: Describe the importance of "postural ergonomics" in the context of workspace design.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe postural ergonomics as the design of workspaces that support natural body alignment to reduce strain on muscles and joints. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that good postural ergonomics reduces fatigue, discomfort, and the risk of long-term musculoskeletal injuries during prolonged work. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>
Award a maximum of 2 marks.  

Award 1 mark for describing postural ergonomics and 1 mark for explaining its importance in workspace design.  

Do not award marks for vague references to comfort without linking to posture or ergonomics.
</note>

parts.content: Describe two physiological benefits and two potential problems of prolonged standing while working.
parts.markscheme:
Identify physiological benefits such as:
- Increased muscle activation in the legs and core, which can improve circulation compared to prolonged sitting. <marks>A1</marks>
- Higher energy expenditure than sitting, which may contribute to reduced sedentary health risks. <marks>A1</marks>

Describe potential physiological problems such as:
- Prolonged load on the lower limbs which can lead to fatigue, joint strain, or development of varicose veins. <marks>A1</marks>
- Extended standing which can cause lower back pain due to sustained spinal loading and poor posture. <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>
Award a maximum of 4 marks. Award 1 mark for each clearly described physiological benefit or problem linked to prolonged standing. Credit up to 2 marks for benefits and up to 2 marks for problems. 
	
Do not award marks for non-physiological issues such as productivity or comfort unless clearly linked to bodily effects.
</note>

parts.content: Analyse how sit–stand workstation design can address the issues identified.
parts.markscheme:
Indicative content may include:

- Analyse how height-adjustable desks allow users to alternate between sitting and standing, reducing prolonged static posture. <marks>A1</marks>
- Analyse how alternating posture reduces continuous muscle fatigue associated with long periods of standing. <marks>A1</marks>
- Analyse how alternating posture reduces health risks linked to prolonged sitting by increasing movement and muscle activation. <marks>A1</marks>
- Analyse how anti-fatigue mats reduce pressure on the feet and lower limbs during standing work. <marks>A1</marks>
- Analyse how adjustable monitor and keyboard positioning helps maintain neutral spinal and wrist posture in both sitting and standing positions. <marks>A1</marks>
- Analyse how sit–stand designs encourage regular movement and posture change, improving circulation and reducing discomfort. <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>
Award a maximum of 6 marks. 
	
Award 1 mark for each distinct analytical point that explains how a sit–stand workstation design feature addresses issues of prolonged sitting or standing. 

Points must go beyond simple description and show a clear link between the design feature and reduction of physiological problems or maintenance of benefits.
</note>

parts.content: Evaluate ergonomic requirements for accessories that support effective sit–stand workstation use.
parts.markscheme:
Indicative content may include:

- Anti-fatigue mats that provide cushioning reduce pressure on feet and lower limbs during prolonged standing. 
- Poorly designed mats can be unstable or create trip hazards, reducing overall safety. 
- Monitor arms that allow easy height and distance adjustment support neutral neck posture in both sitting and standing. 
- Fixed or poorly adjustable monitors can increase neck strain, undermining sit–stand benefits. 
- Adjustable keyboard trays support neutral wrist and shoulder posture across different working heights. 
- Lack of adjustability can lead to wrist extension or shoulder elevation, increasing fatigue. 
- Footrests or foot rails enable weight shifting, reducing lower back and leg discomfort. 
- Accessories that are intuitive and quick to adjust encourage regular posture change rather than static working. 

8 marks total

<note>
**Level Descriptors**

**1–2 marks**: The response identifies a limited number of accessories with simple statements about comfort or support. Little or no evaluation is present, and links to effective sit–stand use are weak or implicit.

**3–4 marks**: The response describes several accessories and makes some links to ergonomic needs. There is limited evaluation, with some consideration of how design features improve or hinder sit–stand workstation effectiveness.

**5–6 marks**: The response analyses multiple accessories in a balanced way, explaining how specific ergonomic requirements support posture change, comfort, or injury prevention. Some limitations or trade-offs are considered.

**7–8 marks**: The response provides a clear, well-structured evaluation of ergonomic requirements across a range of accessories. It consistently links design features to effective sit–stand use, considers both benefits and limitations, and demonstrates strong understanding of ergonomic principles in context.
</note>

questions.specification: A startup is developing a sustainable urban commuting package that includes a lightweight adjustable bicycle, an ergonomic helmet, and a multi-purpose backpack. The goal is to encourage more people to cycle to work safely and comfortably. The design team wants to ensure all components are ergonomically optimised.

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e2a34cdd-098a-4c53-946e-a76805183302)

Identify one anthropometric consideration used when designing the bicycle seat and explain its importance.
parts.markscheme:
- Seat height must align with leg length (e.g., inside leg measurement). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensures efficient pedalling and avoids knee strain or overextension. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how adjustability improves the ergonomic performance of the bicycle for a diverse user population.
parts.markscheme:
- Seat height, handlebar angle, and frame size can be adjusted. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accommodates 5th–95th percentile riders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables correct posture and reduces fatigue. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages inclusivity for different ages, genders, and body types. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b3ffe09e-826e-4d90-9adf-70aacfe75dc7)

Describe how physiological factors may have influenced the helmet’s internal protective structure (e.g., liner/foam, padding, and ventilation channels).
parts.markscheme:
- Padding reduces pressure on skull and absorbs shock. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Airflow channels reduce overheating and sweat accumulation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adjustable straps improve fit and stability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Weight distribution avoids neck strain. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how psychological ergonomics may impact how consistently the helmet is used.
parts.markscheme:
- Appealing design and colors improve user acceptance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clear fastening mechanism increases perceived safety. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Comfortable fit leads to positive user association. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Easy to adjust = intuitive use and higher compliance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4390b031-6943-4a5e-927c-14e08bae9a02)

Discuss how both anthropometric and physiological considerations influence the design of the backpack’s strap-and-support system.
parts.markscheme:
- **Anthropometric factors:** Strap length must fit torso width and shoulder breadth. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adjustable waist/chest straps accommodate various body sizes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Physiological factors:** Padded straps reduce pressure on shoulders and nerves. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ventilated back panel reduces sweating. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Even weight distribution avoids back and neck strain. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Quick-release buckles accommodate reduced finger dexterity (e.g. gloves). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- *Award up to [6 max]; at least one point from each category.*

questions.specification: Why would a product need to be adjustable in design?

options.content: To suit different percentile users and increase inclusivity
To reduce cognitive load on users
To avoid manufacturing errors
To increase its weight and stability

questions.specification:
Which design principle is being applied in the product shown?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/d5e20a3a-865f-4e33-bdd4-fe6e15d55db7.jpeg)

options.content: Biomimicry
options.markscheme: Biomimicry refers to the design and production of materials, structures, and systems that are modeled on biological entities and processes. The chair shown does not exhibit features derived from natural forms.

options.content: Adjustability to suit various users
options.markscheme: The product shown is an ergonomic chair with visible mechanical features such as levers and adjustable armrests. These features allow the product to be tailored to different user sizes (anthropometrics), which is a key principle of adjustable design.

options.content: Fashion-based aesthetics
options.markscheme: While the chair has a modern appearance, its primary design focus is functional ergonomics rather than fleeting fashion trends or purely aesthetic ornamentation.

options.content: Integrated digital systems
options.markscheme: Integrated digital systems would involve sensors, smart connectivity, or electronic interfaces. The chair shown relies on mechanical adjustments rather than digital technology.

questions.specification: What is the main concern of anthropometrics?

options.content: Biochemical reactions to long-term usage
Human physical measurements within percentile ranges
Emotional response to auditory feedback
Psychological analysis of colour theory

questions.specification: Which of the following best explains the benefit of adjustability in ergonomic product design?

options.content: It increases production complexity and cost
It ensures comfort and functionality across a broader user population
It eliminates the impact of psychological factors
It removes the need for anthropometric testing

questions.specification: Which of the following is an example of a population stereotype in user-centred design?

options.content: Designing based on the belief that red means "stop"
Collecting feedback only from expert users
Using recycled materials in all components
Assuming all users prefer eco-friendly packaging

questions.specification: What is the primary concern when designing the workspace envelope for an industrial robot operated by humans?

options.content: The lighting conditions during operation
The maximum and minimum reach of the human operator
The cost of materials required
The speed of production output

questions.specification: Why are percentile measurements used in anthropometric data?

options.content: To select data that accommodates the majority of users
To calculate software response times
To select the appropriate environmental materials
To determine the average heart rate

questions.specification: Topic 1: Human factors and ergonomics - Psychological factors

parts.content: Explain how psychological factors influence the design of a vehicle dashboard.
parts.markscheme:
- **Visual perception**: affects the layout of displays and the selection of indicator colors (e.g., red for warnings) to ensure rapid and accurate recognition of information. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Auditory feedback**: alerts such as seatbelt warnings or lane-departure pings improve response time by signaling critical information through sound. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Cognitive load**: controls need to be logically grouped and prioritised to reduce the driver's mental effort and prevent cognitive overload during operation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Visual clarity**: the use of clear font sizes and high-contrast, standardised symbols reduces driver distraction by allowing for quick glancing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Tactile feedback**: haptic responses, such as vibration on buttons or textured surfaces, enhance user control by providing physical confirmation of actions without requiring visual focus. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Multi-sensory cues**: supporting users with different sensory strengths (e.g., visual and auditory cues for a turn signal) ensures the interface is accessible and reduces the chance of error. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Define ergonomics and state one purpose of applying it in product design.

parts.content: Define ergonomics and state one purpose of applying it in product design.
parts.markscheme:
* defining ergonomics as the application of scientific information concerning the relationship between human beings and the design of objects, systems and environments <marks>A1</marks>
* stating one purpose of applying ergonomics, such as to improve comfort, efficiency, safety, or usability <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>
Award [1] for a basic definition and [1] for a relevant aim or function, up to [2 marks].
</note>

questions.specification: When designing a workstation for a factory setting, which percentile data is most appropriate for the reach envelope of controls?

options.content: $5^{\text{th}}$ percentile, to guarantee access for the smallest users
options.markscheme: Design for reach uses the $5^{\text{th}}$ percentile (representing the smallest or shortest users) to ensure that controls are within reach for the largest possible portion of the population. If the person with the shortest reach can access the control, then all users with a longer reach ($50^{\text{th}}$ and $95^{\text{th}}$ percentiles) will also be able to do so.

options.content: A custom average based on male anthropometric data only
$95^{\text{th}}$ percentile, to ensure usability by the largest users
$50^{\text{th}}$ percentile, to accommodate average reach

questions.specification: What area does physiology study in the context of design?

options.content: Brand perception of health-related products
User satisfaction with UI aesthetics
AI automation of user feedback
Muscle memory and posture in use of systems

questions.specification:
Which combination of human factors is most critical to ensure comfort and usability in the VR system shown? 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/aa4a9f2a-49b0-4263-91e4-4bcabeff48f4)

options.content: Material strength and AI training
Ambient sound and lighting
Psychology and physiology
Data encryption and haptics

questions.specification:
What ergonomics concept is illustrated in the diagram?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/d3cf0e8d-3eeb-4bca-939d-ff12e7da3873.jpeg)

options.content: Work envelope and reach
options.markscheme: Correct. The diagram illustrates the areas on a workstation reachable by a user (normal and maximum reach), which together define the 'work envelope'.

options.content: Colour psychology
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Colour psychology relates to the impact of colour on human behaviour and mood, which is not represented here.

options.content: Component prototyping
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Component prototyping involves creating early models of a product's parts, whereas this is an ergonomic analysis diagram.

options.content: Airflow testing
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Airflow testing involves analyzing fluid dynamics or ventilation, which is not shown in this plan-view reach diagram.

questions.specification:
A design consultancy has created a line of ergonomic products for public gyms aimed at reducing strain, improving usability, and supporting user wellbeing. These include an adjustable rowing machine handle, a color-coded dumbbell rack, and a touchscreen-controlled treadmill interface.

See **Figures 1**, **2** and **3**.

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/22f742cd-1e57-4ce5-b350-e52f7fa760c9)

Figure 1 shows an ergonomic design of a rowing machine handle. Identify two ways the ergonomic design of the handle improves the user’s physical experience.
parts.markscheme:
- Contoured shape fits hand comfortably to reduce blisters. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Soft grip material reduces vibration and improves grip. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how anthropometric and physiological considerations influenced the design of the handle grip.
parts.markscheme:
- Anthropometric: Diameter fits average hand sizes from percentile data. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Physiological: Grip texture enhances muscle control. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports neutral wrist position to reduce fatigue. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c31b7d2d-3548-47e3-81f7-16a7947c4dde)

Describe two ergonomic advantages of using color-coded dumbbells in a public gym setting.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies an ergonomic advantage of colour coding (e.g. aids quick visual identification / reduces cognitive load / supports inclusivity for limited literacy or language skills / reduces errors when choosing weights). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how this advantage improves ergonomics in a public gym setting (e.g. faster selection, reduced confusion, improved safety/usability). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a second ergonomic advantage of colour coding (e.g. aids quick visual identification / reduces cognitive load / supports inclusivity for limited literacy or language skills / reduces errors when choosing weights). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how this second advantage improves ergonomics in a public gym setting (e.g. faster selection, reduced confusion, improved safety/usability). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0d56a0e4-e8ac-4145-83d1-0a1191d86d78)

Discuss how psychological and physiological ergonomics are addressed in the touchscreen interface design for a treadmill.
parts.markscheme:
- Psychological ergonomics: Clear icons reduce confusion and improve user confidence. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Psychological ergonomics: Color contrast enhances readability for all ages. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Psychological ergonomics: Touch feedback provides reassurance of selection. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Physiological ergonomics: Interface height matches average standing eye level (static data). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Physiological ergonomics: Buttons sized to match fingertip dimensions for comfortable use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Physiological ergonomics: Responsive UI minimizes need for excessive arm movement. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- *Award up to [6 max]; minimum one point from each ergonomic category.* <marks>0 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain two potential ergonomic problems that could arise if the touchscreen interface is poorly designed.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies one potential ergonomic problem (e.g. screen too high/low causing neck/shoulder strain; small/closely spaced icons causing inaccurate selections/finger fatigue; poor contrast/glare limiting visibility; slow/non-responsive UI causing frustration). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains the impact on the user (e.g. discomfort/strain, increased error rate, fatigue, reduced usability/safety). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a second potential ergonomic problem (e.g. screen too high/low causing neck/shoulder strain; small/closely spaced icons causing inaccurate selections/finger fatigue; poor contrast/glare limiting visibility; slow/non-responsive UI causing frustration). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains the impact on the user (e.g. discomfort/strain, increased error rate, fatigue, reduced usability/safety). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- *Award [1] for identification and [1] for explanation for each well-explained ergonomic issue up to [4 max].* <marks>0 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which of the following is most relevant to the concept of adjustability?

options.content: Colour matching in UX design
Digital responsiveness of sensors
Standard unit sizing for electronics
Reach and clearance variation among users

questions.specification:
What does this distribution help designers determine?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXeV3MQnQvlrxoshHKJhtn5sVt-qQhjTizPoPM3rWS3lfQ_XqrBgQggSxU-rE8g4ZXor0gmrvt_Ix2IR4Wfnvh266pIqIpsoD0O68fjUPf74svuVIZfYnVzN-a4BX6vAZKsoS63c?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Adjustability of helmet sizes
options.markscheme: The normal distribution curve represents anthropometric data. Designers use this data to determine percentiles (e.g., 5th to 95th) to ensure products like helmets have sufficient adjustability to fit the majority of the population.

options.content: Safety ratings for vehicles
Anthropometric cut-off points for design (e.g., designing for the 5th–95th percentile range)
Determining the mean and standard deviation of body measurements to size products appropriately

questions.specification: Psychological ergonomics (cognitive ergonomics) focuses on mental processes and how humans interact with systems through their senses and perception.

parts.content: State two senses considered in psychological ergonomics and explain their relevance.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify **vision** as a sense and explain its relevance (e.g., color contrast, warning symbols, or legibility of displays) <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify **touch** (haptics) as a sense and explain its relevance (e.g., tactile feedback, texture, or temperature cues) <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify **hearing** as a sense and explain its relevance (e.g., auditory signals or alarms for feedback) <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify **smell** as a sense and explain its relevance (e.g., scent added to gas to indicate a leak) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each valid sense identified with a brief explanation of its relevance, up to a maximum of [2].</note>
